செயல்முறை / கண்டறிதல்கள்: நோயாளிக்கு நீரிழிவு நரம்பு பாதிப்பால் ஏற்பட்ட, நீண்டகால காசநோயின் ஒரு சிக்கலாக, இடது பாதத்தில் நாள்பட்ட, ஆறாத புண் இருந்தது. அறுவை சிகிச்சைக்கு முந்தைய பரிசோதனைகளில், எக்ஸ்-ரே மற்றும் எம்.ஆர்.ஐ. ஆகியவை எலும்பு பாதிப்பை உறுதிப்படுத்தின. இடது முழங்காலுக்குக் கீழே துண்டிக்கப்படும் அறுவை சிகிச்சை (BKA) நீண்ட பின்புற ஃபிளாப் நுட்பத்தைப் பயன்படுத்தி செய்யப்பட்டது. அறுவை சிகிச்சையின் போது, விரிவான எலும்பு அழிவு மற்றும் சீக்வெஸ்ட்ரம் உருவாக்கம் கண்டறியப்பட்டது, அவை அகற்றப்பட்டன. டிபியல் டியூபரோசிட்டி மட்டத்தில் அறுவை சிகிச்சை முடிக்கப்பட்டது. நோயாளி இந்தச் செயல்முறையை நன்றாக ஏற்றுக்கொண்டார். அறுவை சிகிச்சைக்குப் பிந்தைய கட்டு போடப்பட்டது, முழங்காலுக்குக் கீழே ஒரு ஸ்பிளிண்ட் வைக்கப்பட்டது. கண்காணிப்பிற்காக நோயாளி அறுவை சிகிச்சைக்குப் பிந்தைய வார்டுக்கு மாற்றப்பட்டார்.
[[BED_NUMBER|12]] [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]] [[RELATIVE_NAME|Sowmya Nan]]
generated_text_en:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Government Medical College Hospital, Vellore]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Harish Nan]] [[AGE|48]] [[GENDER|Male]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Venkatesh Iyer]]
Procedure / findings: Patient presented with a chronic, non-healing left foot ulcer with underlying osteomyelitis, secondary to diabetic neuropathy, a known complication of his long-standing tuberculosis. Pre-operative investigations, including an X-ray and MRI, confirmed bone involvement. The procedure performed was a left below-knee amputation (BKA) with a long posterior flap technique to ensure adequate soft tissue coverage. Intra-operative findings revealed extensive bone destruction and sequestrum formation, which were debrided. The amputation was completed at the level of the tibial tuberosity. The patient tolerated the procedure well. Post-operative dressing was applied, and a below-knee splint was placed. The patient was shifted to the post-operative ward for monitoring.
[[BED_NUMBER|12]] [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]] [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]] [[RELATIVE_NAME|Sowmya Nan]]

persona: Srinivas Prasad, they blend a routine-driven, community-focused mindset with a gregarious love for cricket, festivals, and thrift, balancing modest ambitions with a steadfast devotion to family and faith.
cultural_background: Srinivas Prasad comes from the Telugu‑speaking rural heartland of Karimnagar in Telangana. He values routine, respects elder authority, and maintains close ties with his extended family and neighbours.
linguistic_background: Although Srinivas never attended formal school and remains illiterate, he is fluent in spoken Telugu, which he uses for daily communication and business. He can recognise simple written Telugu signs, price labels and basic invoices with help, and he has a functional grasp of spoken Hindi encountered in wholesale markets. His reading ability is limited to numbers and common symbols, so he relies heavily on oral instructions and visual cues.
skills_and_expertise: Srinivas has built a solid skill set over decades in wholesale trade. He possesses practical knowledge of market trends in agricultural produce and consumer goods, plans delivery routes efficiently, and handles basic bookkeeping tasks. His communication skills enable him to resolve disputes calmly and foster trust among traders.

hobbies_and_interests: Srinivas enjoys participating in community gatherings, especially during temple festivals where he sings bhajans with friends. hobbies_and_interests_list: ["Community festivals", "Bhajan singing", "Telugu movies", "Cricket", "Card games (Rummy)", "Vegetable gardening", "Storytelling to grandchildren"]
career_goals_and_ambitions: Srinivas aims to secure a stable income for his family as he approaches retirement, hoping to expand his network of wholesale contacts and perhaps open his own small retail outlet. He wishes to mentor younger sales staff, ensuring they inherit his practical knowledge. A key ambition is to provide his children and grandchildren with better educational opportunities, even as he remains rooted in his traditional values.

system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

user_prompt:
Write one complete OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Telugu (code=te, script=Telugu). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 60
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Sales Supervisor, Wholesale Trade
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Telangana
- district: Karimnagar
- first_language: Telugu
- clinical_persona_summary: Mr. Srinivas Prasad, a 60-year-old male sales supervisor in wholesale trade, is currently married and resides in rural Karimnagar, Telangana. He is illiterate but fluent in Telugu, and his lifestyle is routine-driven with a focus on family and community. His daily activities include walking, watching cricket, and managing his small sales team.

Document format guide for OPD Slip (Outpatient Department) (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton (adapt language/script; keep tags):
OPD Slip | [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital X]] | ID [[HOSPITAL_ID|DH-X-01]]
Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|...]] | DOB [[DOB|1992-03-14]] | Age: [[AGE|34]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Female]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-001]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. ...]]
District: [[DISTRICT|...]] | Chief complaint: <short clinical line>

MANDATORY inline entities (13 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOB — Date of Birth e.g. [[DOB|1992-03-14]]
- OCCUPATION — Occupation e.g. [[OCCUPATION|Teacher]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- HOSPITAL_ID — Hospital ID e.g. [[HOSPITAL_ID|HSP-UP-014]]
- DISTRICT — District e.g. [[DISTRICT|Kanpur Nagar]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- PHONE_NUMBER — Phone Number e.g. [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876512340]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- EMPLOYEE_ID — Employee ID e.g. [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

Patient: [[PATIENT_NAME|Srinivas Prasad]] | DOB [[DOB|1964-07-22]] | Age: [[AGE|60]] | Gender: [[GENDER|Male]]
Occupation: [[OCCUPATION|Sales Supervisor, Wholesale Trade]] | MRN: [[MRN|OPD-2024-0822-004]] | Doctor: [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Rajesh Kumar]]
District: [[DISTRICT|Karimnagar]] | Chief complaint: Persistent cough for 3 weeks with low-grade fever and weight loss.
History of Present Illness: Patient reports a productive cough, initially dry, now with occasional yellowish sputum. He has experienced evening fevers and a 5 kg weight loss over the past month. He denies any hemoptysis or chest pain. He is a known hypertensive on medication.
Physical Examination: Vitals: BP 130/80 mmHg, Pulse 88/min, Temp 99.4°F, SpO2 96% on room air. General: Thin, cachectic male, no pallor. Respiratory: Chest expansion reduced on the right side, dullness to percussion in the right lower zone, bronchial breath sounds heard. Cardiovascular: S1 and S2 are audible, no murmurs.
Investigations: Sputum for AFB was sent on 15/08/2024. Chest X-ray shows right-sided pleural effusion and infiltrates.
Diagnosis: Pulmonary Tuberculosis with Right Pleural Effusion.
Treatment Plan: Started on Category I DOTS regimen. Patient counselled on treatment adherence and nutrition. Follow-up in 2 weeks.
Relative Contact: [[RELATIVE_NAME|Lakshmi Prasad]] | Phone: [[PHONE_NUMBER|9876543210]]
Employee ID: [[EMPLOYEE_ID|EMP-KL-7890]]

professional_persona: Shrinu Charugundla, a 70‑year‑old market stall operator in West Godavari, leverages decades of oral negotiation, rapid mental maths and storytelling to attract customers, while they are sociable, talkative and love routine, making their stall a lively community hub.
linguistic_persona: Shrinu Charugundla, a native Telugu speaker who despite being illiterate, relies on fluent spoken Telugu and functional English learned from tourists and traders to negotiate prices, and they sign their name on simple tags while understanding basic instructions using gestures and mental arithmetic instead of written records.
sports_persona: Shrinu Charugundla follows cricket passionately, never misses a match on the village TV, cheerfully roots for the Indian national team and the Hyderabad Sunrisers, and joins a weekly evening walk with fellow market vendors to stay active, though they sometimes skip the walk due to market overtime.
arts_persona: Shrinu Charugundla enjoys listening to traditional Burra Katha folk performances, often humming along while arranging their stall, and is a fan of legendary Telugu film actor Chiranjeevi and playback singer S. P. Balasubrahmanyam, whose songs they play on their old audio player during breaks.
travel_persona: Shrinu Charugundla rarely travels far, but enjoys occasional trips to nearby Tirupati for pilgrimage, relishes weekend outings to the Alampur riverbank with their grandchildren, and prefers simple train journeys over bus rides, though they sometimes skip travel plans due to market commitments.
culinary_persona: Shrinu Charugundla prefers hearty Andhra dishes such as tangy Pulihora, crisp Pesarattu, and spicy Gongura pachadi, enjoys cooking them for family gatherings, but occasionally forgets to add the right amount of tamarind, leading to a slightly sour taste, and loves sipping strong filter coffee after meals.
persona: Shrinu Charugundla is a talkative, routine‑loving 70‑year‑old market veteran who blends sharp mental maths with folk humor, yet often worries about finances and forgets a bill here and there.
cultural_background: Shrinu belongs to the Telugu‑speaking agrarian community of West Godavari in coastal Andhra Pradesh. He grew up in a joint family that follows traditional customs, celebrates festivals such as Sankranti, Ugadi and Vijayadashami with great zeal, and respects the rhythms of rural life. The weekly market where he works is a social hub; his daily routine of setting up the stall, greeting regular customers and bargaining over produce reflects the deep‑rooted cultural emphasis on community, hospitality and practical simplicity.
linguistic_background: He is a native Telugu speaker, using the language for everyday conversation, storytelling and negotiating with customers. Although illiterate, he recognises many Telugu letters and can sign his name, but cannot read or write fluently. He has acquired spoken English through interactions with tourists and fellow traders, enough to understand basic instructions and explain prices in English, though he rarely writes in English. His communication relies on oral proficiency, body language and mental arithmetic rather than written documentation.
skills_and_expertise: He has honed practical sales skills over decades, excelling in oral negotiation, quick mental calculations, and building rapport with regular customers. His expertise includes sourcing fresh produce and household items, arranging the stall attractively, and using storytelling to draw attention. He can read simple price tags and manage inventory mentally, and his assertive yet friendly demeanor helps him secure favourable deals. His ability to entertain through jokes and anecdotes makes his stall a popular gathering spot.
skills_and_expertise_list: ['Oral sales', 'Bargaining', 'Customer relationship building', 'Mental arithmetic', 'Product sourcing', 'Stall arrangement', 'Storytelling', 'Inventory management in mind']
hobbies_and_interests: Shrinu loves listening to folk songs like Burra Katha performances, playing card games such as Satati with his friends, and cooking traditional Andhra meals like Pulihora and Pesarattu for family gatherings. He enjoys watching cricket matches on the village TV, tending to a small vegetable garden beside his house, and sharing anecdotes and jokes that make his stall a lively spot. He also volunteers to help organize local festivals, assisting in setting up decorative rangoli and lighting.
hobbies_and_interests_list: ['Listening to folk music', 'Storytelling', 'Card games', 'Cooking traditional meals', 'Watching cricket', 'Gardening', 'Participating in festivals']
career_goals_and_ambitions: Even at 70, Shrinu aims to keep his stall thriving and maintain a steady income to support his wife and grandchildren. He wishes to pass on his market wisdom to younger family members, perhaps mentoring a son or grandson who can take over the business. He hopes to upgrade his stall with a modest canopy and better storage, and to save enough for a comfortable retirement where he can spend his evenings attending temple rituals without financial worries. He also dreams of preserving the traditional market culture for future generations.
persona_clinical_summary: A 70-year-old male from West Godavari, Andhra Pradesh, who is currently married and works as a market stall salesperson with an illiterate education level. He is a native Telugu speaker from a rural agrarian background, with a routine-loving and talkative personality who often worries about finances. Clinically, he may have mild cognitive concerns related to forgetfulness and financial anxiety.
system_prompt:
You generate synthetic Indian clinical documents with inline surrogate PHI/PII annotations for dataset creation under DPDP-safe research use.

Inline annotation format: [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]
Every PII/PHI mention in the generated clinical text MUST be wrapped as [[ENTITY_TYPE|surrogate_value]] using an entity id from the allow-list only. Do not emit unlabeled real-looking identifiers. Do not invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, MEDICAL_RECORD_NUMBER — use MRN / APPOINTMENT_ID / etc.).

Rules:
- Use ONLY synthetic surrogate values. Never copy real people or real IDs.
- Keep the same surrogate for the same entity type within one document (DICS).
- Write clinical prose in the assigned document language and script.
- ID-like entity values (Aadhaar, PAN, IFSC, MRN, phone, policy numbers, emails) MAY stay Latin/digits even in Indic documents — that is expected, not impurity.
- Person/place names should prefer the document script when natural; Latin names are OK if culturally plausible.
- Ground geography in the persona state/district/zone when location appears.
- Age and gender in the note MUST match the persona fields exactly.
- Clinical content MUST fit the assigned domain AND the persona (no maternal delivery for male patients; no paediatric domain for adults).
- Include every mandatory entity from the doc-type profile at least once (required ∪ optional when require_all_profile_entities is on).
- Prefer exactly one tag per mandatory TYPE. Never dump/repeat the same TYPE to pad coverage (max 2 occurrences). No trailing entity laundry-lists.
- Device/network IDs (IP_ADDRESS, URL, IMEI_NUMBER, MAC_ADDRESS) belong only in telemedicine session metadata — once each, never inside chat turns.
- VEHICLE_REGISTRATION belongs only in ER arrival, hospital billing, or insurance claim lines — once, never dumped.
- Optional entities may be included when natural for the document type.
- Do not invent entity TYPE names outside the provided allow-list.
- Never invent DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER — write calendar dates/times as plain prose outside tags.
- Do not wrap non-PHI clinical prose (symptoms, meds, vitals, drug names) as entities.
- Never tag currency/amounts (rupees, GST, totals, line-item rates) — leave as plain numbers.
- Use 10-digit Indian mobiles (optional +91 / 91 prefix). Avoid toy patterns.
- automated_sms must stay short (≤ ~400 characters, 2–4 sentences) — never dump a full chart into an SMS.
- Output ONLY the clinical document text — no markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.

ENTITY TYPE ALLOW-LIST (use ONLY these TYPE names):
AADHAAR_NUMBER, ABHA_ADDRESS, ABHA_ID, ADMISSION_NUMBER, AGE, APPOINTMENT_ID, ASHA_WORKER_NAME, BANK_ACCOUNT_NUMBER, BANK_ROUTING_NUMBER, BED_NUMBER, BPL_RATION_CARD, CASTE, CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER, CVV, DISTRICT, DOB, DOCTOR_NAME, DRIVING_LICENCE, EMAIL_ADDRESS, EMPLOYEE_ID, ENCOUNTER_ID, GENDER, HOSPITAL_ID, HOSPITAL_NAME, IFSC_CODE, IMEI_NUMBER, INSURANCE_POLICY_NUMBER, IP_ADDRESS, MAC_ADDRESS, MRN, OCCUPATION, PAN_NUMBER, PASSPORT_NUMBER, PATIENT_ID, PATIENT_NAME, PHONE_NUMBER, PIN, PIN_CODE, REFERRAL_ID, RELATIVE_NAME, RELIGION, RESIDENTIAL_ADDRESS, STUDENT_ID, TAX_ID, TELEPHONE_LANDLINE, URL, VEHICLE_REGISTRATION, VILLAGE, VOTER_ID, WARD_NUMBER

user_prompt:
Write one complete Surgical / Operative Note for clinical domain Tuberculosis & Non-Communicable Diseases.

GENERATION LANGUAGE: English (Latin script) — this is an English pivot draft. A later pipeline stage will translate clinical prose into Telugu (code=te, script=Telugu). Keep [[TYPE|value]] tags intact and valid.
ID-like tagged values stay Latin/digits. Drug names / labs stay untagged.

Persona anchors (must remain consistent; patient = this persona):
- sex / gender: Male
- age: 70
- marital_status: Currently Married
- occupation: Stall and Market Salespersons, Other
- education_level: Illiterate
- zone: Rural
- state: Andhra Pradesh
- district: West Godavari
- first_language: Telugu
- clinical_persona_summary: A 70-year-old male from West Godavari, Andhra Pradesh, who is currently married and works as a market stall salesperson with an illiterate education level. He is a native Telugu speaker from a rural agrarian background, with a routine-loving and talkative personality who often worries about finances. Clinically, he may have mild cognitive concerns related to forgetfulness and financial anxiety.

Document format guide for Surgical / Operative Note (English structure; a later stage translates to the target language):
Example skeleton:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|...]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|...]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|...]] [[AGE|50]] [[GENDER|Female]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|...]]
Procedure / findings: MUST describe an actual procedure/operation matching the
assigned clinical domain (not a psychiatry admission note). Keep relative names
gender-plausible.

MANDATORY inline entities (11 types) — EACH must appear ≥ once as [[TYPE|surrogate_value]]. Missing ANY is a hard failure:
- PATIENT_NAME — Patient Name e.g. [[PATIENT_NAME|Meena Devi]]
- AGE — Age e.g. [[AGE|34]]
- GENDER — Gender e.g. [[GENDER|Female]]
- DOCTOR_NAME — Doctor Name e.g. [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. Anjali Sharma]]
- HOSPITAL_NAME — Hospital / Institution Name e.g. [[HOSPITAL_NAME|District Hospital Kanpur]]
- ADMISSION_NUMBER — Admission Number e.g. [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]]
- WARD_NUMBER — Ward Number e.g. [[WARD_NUMBER|B2]]
- BED_NUMBER — Bed Number e.g. [[BED_NUMBER|12]]
- MRN — Medical Record Number (MRN) e.g. [[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
- RELATIVE_NAME — Relative Name (Father/Mother/Spouse/Guardian) e.g. [[RELATIVE_NAME|Ramesh Devi]]
- PATIENT_ID — Patient ID e.g. [[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]

Before finishing, mentally checklist every mandatory TYPE above.
Do NOT invent TYPE names (no DATE, TIME, APPOINTMENT_DATE, APPOINTMENT_TIME, POLICY_NUMBER, TOTAL_AMOUNT, GST_AMOUNT, GRAND_TOTAL). Put dates/times and money amounts in plain prose.
Produce ONLY the clinical document text with inline annotations. No markdown fences, no preamble, no postscript.
generated_text:
శస్త్రచికిత్స గమనిక — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Government General Hospital, West Godavari]] అడ్మిషన్ [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]] వార్డు [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Rao Venkata Subba Reddy]] [[AGE|70]] [[GENDER|Male]] సర్జన్ [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. K. Ramesh]]
విధానం / పరిశీలనలు: రోగి కుడి పార్శ్వ మాలియోలస్ (lateral malleolus) పై దీర్ఘకాలికంగా మానని పుండుతో వచ్చారు, ఇది డిస్టల్ ఫిబులా యొక్క దీర్ఘకాలిక క్షయ ఆస్టియోమైలిటిస్ (tuberculosis osteomyelitis) వల్ల కలిగింది. శస్త్రచికిత్సకు ముందు పరీక్షలలో మంటౌక్స్ పరీక్ష (Mantoux test) పాజిటివ్ వచ్చింది, మరియు ఛాతీ ఎక్స్-రేలో నయమైన పల్మనరీ TB కనిపించింది. రోగికి జనరల్ అనస్థీషియా ఇవ్వబడింది. కుడి దిగువ అవయవానికి టోర్నికెట్ (tourniquet) అమర్చబడింది. కుడి చీలమండ యొక్క పార్శ్వ భాగంలో నిలువు కోత (longitudinal incision) చేయబడింది. డిసెక్షన్ (Dissection) చేసినప్పుడు విస్తృతమైన మచ్చలు మరియు నెక్రోటిక్ ఎముక ముక్కలు కనిపించాయి, ఇవి క్షయ ఆస్టియోమైలిటిస్‌కు అనుగుణంగా ఉన్నాయి. ఫిబులా యొక్క డిస్టల్ 5 సెం.మీ. భాగాన్ని పదునైన పరికరంతో తొలగించారు (resected). ఆ తర్వాత గాయాన్ని నార్మల్ సెలైన్ (normal saline) తో పూర్తిగా కడిగారు. ఎడమ తొడ నుండి స్ప్లిట్-థిక్నెస్ స్కిన్ గ్రాఫ్ట్ (split-thickness skin graft) సేకరించి, కుట్లు మరియు నాన్-అడెహెరెంట్ డ్రెస్సింగ్‌తో లోపలి భాగంపై అమర్చారు. టోర్నికెట్ తొలగించబడింది మరియు రక్తస్రావం నియంత్రణ (hemostasis) సాధించబడింది. రోగి ప్రక్రియను బాగా తట్టుకున్నారు. శస్త్రచికిత్స తర్వాత ప్రణాళికలో 9 నెలల పాటు యాంటీ-ట్యూబర్‌క్యులర్ థెరపీ (ATT) కొనసాగించడం, గాయం సంరక్షణ మరియు ఫిజియోథెరపీ ఉన్నాయి.
[[BED_NUMBER|12]]
[[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
[[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
[[RELATIVE_NAME|Smt. Lakshmi Reddy]]
generated_text_en:
Operative note — [[HOSPITAL_NAME|Government General Hospital, West Godavari]] Adm [[ADMISSION_NUMBER|ADM-2024-0912]] Ward [[WARD_NUMBER|OT]]
[[PATIENT_NAME|Rao Venkata Subba Reddy]] [[AGE|70]] [[GENDER|Male]] Surgeon [[DOCTOR_NAME|Dr. K. Ramesh]]
Procedure / findings: Patient presented with a chronic, non-healing ulcer over the right lateral malleolus, secondary to long-standing tuberculosis osteomyelitis of the distal fibula. Pre-operative investigations included a Mantoux test which was positive, and a chest X-ray showing healed pulmonary TB. The patient was placed under general anesthesia. A tourniquet was applied to the right lower limb. A longitudinal incision was made over the lateral aspect of the right ankle. Dissection revealed extensive scarring and necrotic bone fragments consistent with tuberculous osteomyelitis. The distal 5 cm of the fibula was resected sharply. The wound was then thoroughly irrigated with normal saline. A split-thickness skin graft was harvested from the left thigh and secured over the defect using sutures and a non-adherent dressing. The tourniquet was released, and hemostasis was achieved. The patient tolerated the procedure well. Post-operative plan includes continuation of anti-tubercular therapy (ATT) for a total duration of 9 months, wound care, and physiotherapy.
[[BED_NUMBER|12]]
[[MRN|MRN-2024-0815-001]]
[[PATIENT_ID|PID-77821]]
[[RELATIVE_NAME|Smt. Lakshmi Reddy]]
